This module defines the core [`Schema`] trait for validating structured data.\n4\t//! It provides a generic interface for implementing validation logic across different\n5\t//! data types and validation strategies.\n6\t//!\n7\t//! # Overview\n8\t//!\n9\t//! The [`Schema`] trait defines a contract for validating data structures. Implementors\n10\t//! can define domain-specific validation rules and provide detailed error information\n11\t//! when validation fails.\n12\t//!\n13\t//! # Basic Usage\n14\t//!\n15\t//! ```ignore\n16\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\n17\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n18\t//!\n19\t//! struct PortSchema;\n20\t//!\n21\t//! impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\n22\t//!     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n23\t//!         if *value == 0 {\n24\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port cannot be 0\")\n25\t//!                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n26\t//!         }\n27\t//!         if *value > 65535 {\n28\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port must be between 1 and 65535\")\n29\t//!                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n30\t//!         }\n31\t//!         Ok(())\n32\t//!     }\n33\t//! }\n34\t//! ```\n35\t//!\n36\t//! # Generic Validation\n37\t//!\n38\t//! The Schema trait is generic over the type being validated, allowing you to:\n39\t//!\n40\t//! - Validate primitive types (integers, strings, etc.)\n41\t//! - Validate complex structures (structs, enums)\n42\t//! - Validate collections (vectors, hash maps)\n43\t//! - Compose multiple validators together\n44\t//!\n45\t//! ```ignore\n46\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\n47\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n48\t//!\n49\t//! // Validate a configuration struct\n50\t//! struct ConfigSchema;\n51\t//!\n52\t//! impl Schema<Config> for ConfigSchema {\n53\t//!     fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n54\t//!         // Validate individual fields\n55\t//!         if config.port < 1 || config.port > 65535 {\n56\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port out of range\")\n57\t//!                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n58\t//!         }\n59\t//!         if config.host.is_empty() {\n60\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"host cannot be empty\")\n61\t//!                 .with_path(\"host\"));\n62\t//!         }\n63\t//!         Ok(())\n64\t//!     }\n65\t//! }\n66\t//! ```\n67\t\n68\tuse crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n69\t\n70\t/// Result type for validation operations\n71\t///\n72\t/// This is a type alias for `Result<(), ParseError>`, used throughout\n73\t/// the Schema trait and its implementations. This integrates the Schema\n74\t/// validation with the comprehensive error type hierarchy from the YAML parser.\n75\t///\n76\t/// # Error Types\n77\t///\n78\t/// The [`ParseError`] type provides rich error information including:\n79\t/// - Error categorization (syntax, validation, type mismatch, I/O, etc.)\n80\t/// - Location information (file path, line, column)\n81\t/// - Context messages and code snippets\n82\t/// - Builder pattern for adding contextual information\n83\t///\n84\t/// # Examples\n85\t///\n86\t/// ```\n87\t/// use armor::schema::{Schema, ValidationResult};\n88\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n89\t///\n90\t/// struct PortSchema;\n91\t///\n92\t/// impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\n93\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> ValidationResult {\n94\t///         if *value == 0 {\n95\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port cannot be 0\")\n96\t///                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n97\t///         }\n98\t///         if *value > 65535 {\n99\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port must be between 1 and 65535\")\n100\t///                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n101\t///         }\n102\t///         Ok(())\n103\t///     }\n104\t/// }\n105\t/// ```\n106\tpub type ValidationResult = Result<(), ParseError>;\n107\t\n108\t/// Schema validation trait\n109\t///\n110\t/// This trait defines a generic interface for validating structured data.\n111\t/// Implementors can define domain-specific validation rules for their data types.\n112\t///\n113\t/// # Type Parameters\n114\t///\n115\t/// * `T: ?Sized` - The type being validated (e.g., a struct, primitive type, or collection).\n116\t///   The `?Sized` bound allows the trait to be implemented for both sized and unsized types,\n117\t///   providing maximum flexibility for different validation scenarios.\n118\t///\n119\t/// # Required Methods\n120\t///\n121\t/// Implementors must define the [`validate`](Self::validate) method, which accepts\n122\t/// a reference to a value of type `T` and returns a [`ValidationResult`].\n123\t///\n124\t/// # Examples\n125\t///\n126\t/// ## Validating Primitive Types\n127\t///\n128\t/// ```\n129\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\n130\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n131\t///\n132\t/// struct PositiveIntegerSchema;\n133\t///\n134\t/// impl Schema<i32> for PositiveIntegerSchema {\n135\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n136\t///         if *value <= 0 {\n137\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"must be a positive integer\")\n138\t///                 .with_path(\"value\"));\n139\t///         }\n140\t///         Ok(())\n141\t///     }\n142\t/// }\n143\t///\n144\t/// let schema = PositiveIntegerSchema;\n145\t/// assert!(schema.validate(&42).is_ok());\n146\t/// assert!(schema.validate(&-5).is_err());\n147\t/// ```\n148\t///\n149\t/// ## Validating Structs\n150\t///\n151\t/// ```ignore\n152\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\n153\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n154\t///\n155\t/// struct ServerConfig {\n156\t///     host: String,\n157\t///     port: u16,\n158\t/// }\n159\t///\n160\t/// struct ServerConfigSchema;\n161\t///\n162\t/// impl Schema<ServerConfig> for ServerConfigSchema {\n163\t///     fn validate(&self, config: &ServerConfig) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n164\t///         if config.host.is_empty() {\n165\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"cannot be empty\")\n166\t///                 .with_path(\"host\"));\n167\t///         }\n168\t///         if config.port == 0 {\n169\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"cannot be 0\")\n170\t///                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n171\t///         }\n172\t///         Ok(())\n173\t///     }\n174\t/// }\n175\t/// ```\n176\t///\n177\t/// ## Composing Validators\n178\t///\n179\t/// ```ignore\n180\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\n181\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n182\t///\n183\t/// struct Config {\n184\t///     name: String,\n185\t///     port: u16,\n186\t/// }\n187\t///\n188\t/// // Individual field validators\n189\t/// struct NameSchema;\n190\t/// impl Schema<String> for NameSchema {\n191\t///     fn validate(&self, name: &String) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n192\t///         if name.len() < 3 {\n193\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"must be at least 3 characters\")\n194\t///                 .with_path(\"name\"));\n195\t///         }\n196\t///         Ok(())\n197\t///     }\n198\t/// }\n199\t///\n200\t/// struct PortSchema;\n201\t/// impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\n202\t///     fn validate(&self, port: &u16) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n203\t///         if *port == 0 {\n204\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\"cannot be 0\")\n205\t///                 .with_path(\"port\"));\n206\t///         }\n207\t///         Ok(())\n208\t///     }\n209\t/// }\n210\t///\n211\t/// // Composite validator\n212\t/// struct ConfigSchema;\n213\t/// impl Schema<Config> for ConfigSchema {\n214\t///     fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n215\t///         // Delegate to field-specific validators\n216\t///         NameSchema.validate(&config.name)\n217\t///             .map_err(|e| e.with_path(\"name\"))?;\n218\t///         PortSchema.validate(&config.port)\n219\t///             .map_err(|e| e.with_path(\"port\"))?;\n220\t///         Ok(())\n221\t///     }\n222\t/// }\n223\t/// ```\n224\tpub trait Schema<T: ?Sized> {\n225\t    /// Validate a value according to the schema rules\n226\t    ///\n227\t    /// This method validates the provided value and returns `Ok(())` if validation\n228\t    /// passes, or an error with details about what failed.\n229\t    ///\n230\t    /// # Arguments\n231\t    ///\n232\t    /// * `value` - A reference to the value being validated\n233\t    ///\n234\t    /// # Returns\n235\t    ///\n236\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Validation passed\n237\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Validation failed with error details\n238\t    ///\n239\t    /// # Errors\n240\t    ///\n241\t    /// This method returns an error if:\n242\t    /// - Required fields are missing or empty\n243\t    /// - Values are outside allowed ranges\n244\t    /// - String values don't match required patterns\n245\t    /// - Any other schema constraint is violated\n246\t    ///\n247\t    /// # Examples\n248\t    ///\n249\t    /// ```\n250\t    /// use armor::schema::Schema;\n251\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n252\t    ///\n253\t    /// struct RangeSchema {\n254\t    ///     min: i32,\n255\t    ///     max: i32,\n256\t    /// }\n257\t    ///\n258\t    /// impl Schema<i32> for RangeSchema {\n259\t    ///     fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n260\t    ///         if *value < self.min || *value > self.max {\n261\t    ///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\n262\t    ///                 &format!(\"must be between {} and {}\", self.min, self.max)\n263\t    ///             ).with_path(\"value\"));\n264\t    ///         }\n265\t    ///         Ok(())\n266\t    ///     }\n267\t    /// }\n268\t    ///\n269\t    /// let schema = RangeSchema { min: 1, max: 100 };\n270\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&50).is_ok());\n271\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&0).is_err());\n272\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&101).is_err());\n273\t    /// ```\n274\t    fn validate(&self, value: &T) -> ValidationResult;\n275\t}\n276\t\n277\t#[cfg(test)]\n278\tmod tests {\n279\t    use super::*;\n280\t    use crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n281\t\n282\t    #[test]\n283\t    fn test_parse_error_validation_creation() {\n284\t        let error = ParseError::validation(\"invalid range\")\n285\t            .with_path(\"port\");\n286\t        assert!(error.is_validation());\n287\t        assert_eq!(error.path, Some(\"port\".to_string()));\n288\t    }\n289\t\n290\t    #[test]\n291\t    fn test_parse_error_display() {\n292\t        let error = ParseError::validation(\"must be between 1 and 65535\")\n293\t            .with_path(\"server.port\")\n294\t            .with_line(10);\n295\t        let display = format!(\"{}\", error);\n296\t        assert!(display.contains(\"server.port\"));\n297\t        assert!(display.contains(\"must be between 1 and 65535\"));\n298\t        assert!(display.contains(\"validation error\"));\n299\t    }\n300\t\n301\t    #[test]\n302\t    fn test_schema_trait_basic() {\n303\t        struct PositiveSchema;\n304\t\n305\t        impl Schema<i32> for PositiveSchema {\n306\t            fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> ValidationResult {\n307\t                if *value <= 0 {\n308\t                    return Err(ParseError::validation(\"must be positive\")\n309\t                        .with_path(\"value\"));\n310\t                }\n311\t                Ok(())\n312\t            }\n313\t        }\n314\t\n315\t        let schema = PositiveSchema;\n316\t\n317\t        // Valid values\n318\t        assert!(schema.validate(&1).is_ok());\n319\t        assert!(schema.validate(&100).is_ok());\n320\t\n321\t        // Invalid values\n322\t        let result = schema.validate(&0);\n323\t        assert!(result.is_err());\n324\t        assert!(result.unwrap_err().is_validation());\n325\t\n326\t        let result = schema.validate(&-5);\n327\t        assert!(result.is_err());\n328\t        assert!(result.unwrap_err().is_validation());\n329\t    }\n330\t\n331\t    #[test]\n332\t    fn test_schema_trait_range_validation() {\n333\t        struct RangeSchema {\n334\t            min: i32,\n335\t            max: i32,\n336\t        }\n337\t\n338\t        impl Schema<i32> for RangeSchema {\n339\t            fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_a4643637321f477e9135fe32","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t//! Generic parser traits and interfaces\n2\t//!\n3\t//! This module defines the core parser traits that different parsing\n4\t//! strategies (YAML, JSON, etc.) can implement.\n5\t\n6\tuse std::path::Path;\n7\tuse crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError as YamlParseError;\n8\t\n9\t/// Generic parser trait for different parsing strategies\n10\t///\n11\t/// This trait defines the core interface for parsers that transform\n12\t/// input of one type into structured output. It is designed to be:\n13\t///\n14\t/// 1. **Generic** - Works with any input/output type combination\n15\t/// 2. **Composable** - Multiple parsers can be chained together\n16\t/// 3. **Extensible** - Supports streaming and incremental parsing\n17\t/// 4. **Error-aware** - Uses structured error types for consistent error handling\n18\t///\n19\t/// # Relationship to ParseResult and ParseError\n20\t///\n21\t/// The generic `Parser<Input, Output>` trait uses Rust's standard `Result<Output, ParseError>`\n22\t/// for its core parsing method. However, for YAML-specific parsing, the [`ParseResult<T>`](crate::parsers::yaml::ParseResult)\n23\t/// type provides a richer result structure that includes:\n24\t///\n25\t/// - **Metadata**: Lines processed, bytes processed, timing information\n26\t/// - **Warnings**: Non-fatal issues that don't prevent parsing (e.g., deprecated fields)\n27\t/// - **Detailed error context**: Location information, code snippets, suggestions\n28\t///\n29\t/// ## When to Use Result vs ParseResult\n30\t///\n31\t/// - **Use `Result<T, ParseError>`** (this trait): For generic parsers, simple parsing operations,\n32\t///   and when you want standard Rust error handling patterns\n33\t/// - **Use `ParseResult<T>`** (YAML-specific): For YAML parsing when you need rich metadata,\n34\t///   warning collection, and detailed error reporting\n35\t///\n36\t/// ## Converting Between Result and ParseResult\n37\t///\n38\t/// [`ParseResult<T>`](crate::parsers::yaml::ParseResult) implements `From<Result<T>>` for seamless\n39\t/// integration:\n40\t///\n41\t/// ```ignore\n42\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseResult;\n43\t///\n44\t/// // Convert Result<T, ParseError> to ParseResult<T>\n45\t/// let result: Result<MyType, ParseError> = parse_value();\n46\t/// let parse_result: ParseResult<MyType> = ParseResult::from(result);\n47\t/// ```\n48\t///\n49\t/// # Type Parameters\n50\t///\n51\t/// * `Input` - The input source type (e.g., `&str`, `&[u8]`, `&Path`)\n52\t/// * `Output` - The parsed result type (e.g., configuration struct, AST)\n53\t///\n54\t/// # Trait Bounds\n55\t///\n56\t/// For common use cases, the following type combinations are typical:\n57\t///\n58\t/// - `Input = &str` for string-based formats (YAML, JSON, TOML)\n59\t/// - `Input = &[u8]` for binary formats\n60\t/// - `Input = &Path` for file-based parsing (delegates to string parsing)\n61\t///\n62\t/// The `Output` type is the target representation after parsing.\n63\t///\n64\t/// # Parsing Strategies\n65\t///\n66\t/// This trait supports multiple parsing strategies that can be configured through\n67\t/// [`ParseOptions`] or implementation-specific behavior:\n68\t///\n69\t/// ## Strict Parsing\n70\t///\n71\t/// Strict parsing follows format specifications precisely and rejects any deviations:\n72\t///\n73\t/// ```ignore\n74\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\n75\t///\n76\t/// let parser = StrictParser::new();\n77\t/// let options = ParseOptions::strict();\n78\t/// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\n79\t///\n80\t/// // In strict mode:\n81\t/// // - Unknown fields cause errors\n82\t/// // - Type mismatches are rejected\n83\t/// // - Duplicate keys are not allowed\n84\t/// // - Format specifications are enforced strictly\n85\t/// ```\n86\t///\n87\t/// ## Lenient Parsing\n88\t///\n89\t/// Lenient parsing is more forgiving and attempts to recover from errors:\n90\t///\n91\t/// ```ignore\n92\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\n93\t///\n94\t/// let parser = LenientParser::new();\n95\t/// let options = ParseOptions::lenient();\n96\t/// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\n97\t///\n98\t/// // In lenient mode:\n99\t/// // - Unknown fields are ignored\n100\t/// // - Type coercion is attempted\n101\t/// // - Duplicate keys use last value\n102\t/// // - Minor format issues are tolerated\n103\t/// ```\n104\t///\n105\t/// ## Custom Parsing\n106\t///\n107\t/// Custom parsers implement domain-specific parsing logic:\n108\t///\n109\t/// ```ignore\n110\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n111\t///\n112\t/// struct CustomConfigParser {\n113\t///     allow_extended_syntax: bool,\n114\t///     resolve_variables: bool,\n115\t/// }\n116\t///\n117\t/// impl Parser<&str, CustomConfig> for CustomConfigParser {\n118\t///     fn parse(&self, source: &str) -> Result<CustomConfig, ParseError> {\n119\t///         // Custom parsing logic here\n120\t///         // - Variable substitution\n121\t///         // - Extended syntax support\n122\t///         // - Domain-specific validation\n123\t///         Ok(CustomConfig { /* ... */ })\n124\t///     }\n125\t/// }\n126\t/// ```\n127\t///\n128\t/// # Core Method\n129\t///\n130\t/// The [`Parser::parse()`] method is the core parsing operation:\n131\t///\n132\t/// ```ignore\n133\t/// fn parse(&self, source: Input) -> Result<Output, ParseError>\n134\t/// ```\n135\t///\n136\t/// # Examples\n137\t///\n138\t/// ## Basic String Parsing\n139\t///\n140\t/// ```ignore\n141\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n142\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n143\t///\n144\t/// struct ConfigParser;\n145\t///\n146\t/// struct Config {\n147\t///     name: String,\n148\t///     value: i32,\n149\t/// }\n150\t///\n151\t/// impl Parser<&str, Config> for ConfigParser {\n152\t///     fn parse(&self, source: &str) -> Result<Config, ParseError> {\n153\t///         // Parse logic here\n154\t///         Ok(Config { name: \"test\".to_string(), value: 42 })\n155\t///     }\n156\t/// }\n157\t///\n158\t/// let parser = ConfigParser;\n159\t/// let config = parser.parse(\"name: test\\nvalue: 42\")?;\n160\t/// ```\n161\t///\n162\t/// ## Chaining Parsers\n163\t///\n164\t/// ```ignore\n165\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n166\t///\n167\t/// // Parser 1: Raw string -> Intermediate representation\n168\t/// struct FirstParser;\n169\t/// impl Parser<&str, Intermediate> for FirstParser { /* ... */ }\n170\t///\n171\t/// // Parser 2: Intermediate -> Final output\n172\t/// struct SecondParser;\n173\t/// impl Parser<Intermediate, Output> for SecondParser { /* ... */ }\n174\t///\n175\t/// let first = FirstParser;\n176\t/// let second = SecondParser;\n177\t/// let output = second.parse(first.parse(input)?);\n178\t/// ```\n179\t///\n180\t/// ## Error Handling\n181\t///\n182\t/// ```ignore\n183\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n184\t///\n185\t/// match parser.parse(source) {\n186\t///     Ok(output) => println!(\"Parsed successfully: {:?}\", output),\n187\t///     Err(ParseError::Syntax(msg)) => eprintln!(\"Syntax error: {}\", msg),\n188\t///     Err(ParseError::Io(msg)) => eprintln!(\"I/O error: {}\", msg),\n189\t///     Err(e) => eprintln!(\"Other error: {}\", e),\n190\t/// }\n191\t/// ```\n192\tpub trait Parser<Input, Output> {\n193\t    /// Parse input and return the parsed result\n194\t    ///\n195\t    /// This method accepts input of type `Input` and attempts to parse it\n196\t    /// into a structured `Output` type. Returns `Ok(Output)` on success,\n197\t    /// or `Err(ParseError)` if parsing fails.\n198\t    ///\n199\t    /// # Arguments\n200\t    ///\n201\t    /// * `source` - The input source to parse\n202\t    ///\n203\t    /// # Returns\n204\t    ///\n205\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\n206\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Parsing error with context\n207\t    ///\n208\t    /// # Errors\n209\t    ///\n210\t    /// This method will return an error if:\n211\t    /// - The input syntax is invalid (syntax error)\n212\t    /// - The input cannot be read (I/O error)\n213\t    /// - The input violates constraints (validation error)\n214\t    /// - Types don't match expectations (type mismatch)\n215\t    ///\n216\t    /// # Examples\n217\t    ///\n218\t    /// ```ignore\n219\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n220\t    ///\n221\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n222\t    /// let result = parser.parse(\"key: value\")?;\n223\t    /// ```\n224\t    fn parse(&self, source: Input) -> Result<Output, ParseError>;\n225\t\n226\t    /// Parse input with extended options\n227\t    ///\n228\t    /// This method provides a way to customize parsing behavior through\n229\t    /// options. The default implementation delegates to [`Parser::parse()`].\n230\t    ///\n231\t    /// # Arguments\n232\t    ///\n233\t    /// * `source` - The input source to parse\n234\t    /// * `options` - Parsing options to customize behavior\n235\t    ///\n236\t    /// # Returns\n237\t    ///\n238\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\n239\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Parsing error with context\n240\t    ///\n241\t    /// # Examples\n242\t    ///\n243\t    /// ```ignore\n244\t    /// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\n245\t    ///\n246\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n247\t    /// let options = ParseOptions {\n248\t    ///     strict_mode: true,\n249\t    ///     preserve_comments: false,\n250\t    ///     ..Default::default()\n251\t    /// };\n252\t    /// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\n253\t    /// ```\n254\t    fn parse_with_options(&self, source: Input, options: ParseOptions) -> Result<Output, ParseError> {\n255\t        let _ = options; // Ignore options in default implementation\n256\t        self.parse(source)\n257\t    }\n258\t\n259\t    /// Parse a file at the given path\n260\t    ///\n261\t    /// Convenience method that reads a file and parses its contents.\n262\t    /// The default implementation reads the file and delegates to [`Parser::parse()`].\n263\t    ///\n264\t    /// # Arguments\n265\t    ///\n266\t    /// * `path` - Path to the file to parse\n267\t    ///\n268\t    /// # Returns\n269\t    ///\n270\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\n271\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - I/O or parsing error\n272\t    ///\n273\t    /// # Errors\n274\t    ///\n275\t    /// This method will return an error if:\n276\t    /// - The file cannot be read (I/O error)\n277\t    /// - The file contents are invalid (parsing error)\n278\t    ///\n279\t    /// # Examples\n280\t    ///\n281\t    /// ```ignore\n282\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n283\t    /// use std::path::Path;\n284\t    ///\n285\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n286\t    /// let result = parser.parse_file(Path::new(\"config.yaml\"))?;\n287\t    /// ```\n288\t    fn parse_file(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<Output, ParseError>\n289\t    where\n290\t        Self: Sized,\n291\t        Input: From<String>,\n292\t    {\n293\t        let content = std::fs::read_to_string(path)\n294\t            .map_err(|e| ParseError::Io(e.to_string()))?;\n295\t        self.parse(content.into())\n296\t    }\n297\t\n298\t    /// Validate input without fully parsing\n299\t    ///\n300\t    /// This method performs lightweight validation to check if the input\n301\t    /// is well-formed without constructing the full output structure.\n302\t    /// The default implementation attempts parsing and discards the result.\n303\t    ///\n304\t    /// # Arguments\n305\t    ///\n306\t    /// * `source` - The input source to validate\n307\t    ///\n308\t    /// # Returns\n309\t    ///\n310\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Input is valid\n311\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Input is invalid\n312\t    ///\n313\t    /// # Examples\n314\t    ///\n315\t    /// ```ignore\n316\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n317\t    ///\n318\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n319\t    /// if parser.validate(\"key: value\").is_ok() {\n320\t    ///     println!(\"Input is valid!\");\n321\t    /// }\n322\t    /// ```\n323\t    fn validate(&self, source: Input) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n324\t        self.parse(source)?;\n325\t        Ok(())\n326\t    }\n327\t\n328\t    /// Get metadata about the parser\n329\t    ///\n330\t    /// Returns information about the parser's capabilities and configuration.\n331\t    ///\n332\t    /// # Returns\n333\t    ///\n334\t    /// Parser metadata including supported formats and features\n335\t    ///\n336\t    /// # Examples\n337\t    ///\n338\t    /// ```ignore\n339\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\n340\t    ///\n341\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n342\t    /// let metadata = parser.metadata();\n343\t    /// println!(\"Parser: {}\", metadata.name());\n344\t    /// println!(\"Supports streaming: {}\", metadata.supports_streaming());\n345\t    /// ```\n346\t    fn metadata(&self) -> ParseMetadata {\n347\t        ParseMetadata::default()\n348\t    }\n349\t}\n350\t\n351\t/// Trait for parsers that support streaming input\n352\t///\n353\t/// This trait extends [`Parser`] with capabilities for processing large\n354\t/// inputs as a stream of chunks or multiple sources.\n355\t///\n356\t/// # Examples\n357\t///\n358\t/// ```ignore\n359\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, StreamingParser};\n360\t///\n361\t/// let parser = MyStreamingParser::new();\n362\t///\n363\t/// // Parse multiple sources\n364\t/// let sources = vec![\"file1.yaml\", \"file2.yaml\", \"file3.yaml\"];\n365\t/// let results = parser.parse_stream(sources)?;\n366\t///\n367\t/// for result in results {\n368\t///     println!(\"Parsed: {:?}\", result);\n369\t/// }\n370\t/// ```\n371\tpub trait StreamingParser<Input, Output>: Parser<Input, Output> {\n372\t    /// Parse multiple sources in sequence\n373\t    ///\n374\t    /// This method processes a collection of input sources and returns\n375\t    /// a vector of parsed results. Errors from individual sources are\n376\t    /// collected and returned together.\n377\t    ///\n378\t    /// # Arguments\n379\t    ///\n380\t    /// * `sources` - Iterator of input sources to parse\n381\t    ///\n382\t    /// # Returns\n383\t    ///\n384\t    /// * `Ok(Vec<Output>)` - All sources parsed successfully\n385\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - One or more sources failed to parse\n386\t    ///\n387\t    /// # Examples\n388\t    ///\n389\t    /// ```ignore\n390\t    /// use armor::parsers::StreamingParser;\n391\t    ///\n392\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n393\t    /// let sources = vec![\"config1.yaml\", \"config2.yaml\"];\n394\t    /// let results = parser.parse_stream(sources)?;\n395\t    /// ```\n396\t    fn parse_stream<'a, I>(\n397\t        &self,\n398\t        sources: I,\n399\t    ) -> Result<Vec<Output>, ParseError>\n400\t    where\n401\t        Input: 'a,\n402\t        I: IntoIterator<Item = Input>;\n403\t\n404\t    /// Parse sources in parallel\n405\t    ///\n406\t    /// This method processes multiple sources concurrently using parallel\n407\t    /// execution. Results are collected in the order of the input iterator.\n408\t    ///\n409\t    /// # Arguments\n410\t    ///\n411\t    /// * `sources` - Iterator of input sources to parse\n412\t    /// * `parallelism` - Maximum number of concurrent parse operations\n413\t    ///\n414\t    /// # Returns\n415\t    ///\n416\t    /// * `Ok(Vec<Output>)` - All sources parsed successfully\n417\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - One or more sources failed to parse\n418\t    ///\n419\t    /// # Examples\n420\t    ///\n421\t    /// ```ignore\n422\t    /// use armor::parsers::StreamingParser;\n423\t    ///\n424\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\n425\t    /// let sources = vec![\"file1.yaml\", \"file2.yaml\", \"file3.yaml\"];\n426\t    /// let results = parser.parse_parallel(sources, 4)?;\n427\t    /// ```\n428\t    fn parse_parallel<'a, I>(\n429\t        &self,\n430\t        sources: I,\n431\t        parallelism: usize,\n432\t    ) -> Result<Vec<Output>, ParseError>\n433\t    where\n434\t        Input: 'a,\n435\t        I: IntoIterator<Item = Input>,\n436\t    {\n437\t        // Default implementation processes sequentially\n438\t        self.parse_stream(sources)\n439\t    }\n440\t}\n441\t\n442\t/// Trait for parsers that support incremental parsing\n443\t///\n444\t/// This trait extends [`Parser`] with the ability to parse input in chunks,\n445\t/// allowing processing of large inputs without loading the entire source\n446\t/// into memory.\n447\t///\n448\t/// # Examples\n449\t///\n450\t/// ```ignore\n451\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, IncrementalParser};\n452\t///\n453\t/// let parser = MyIncrementalParser::new();\n454\t///\n455\t/// // Initialize parsing\n456\t/// parser.init_parse()?;\n457\t///\n458\t/// // Feed chunks incrementally\n459\t/// parser.feed_chunk(b\"key: \")?;\n460\t/// parser.feed_chunk(b\"value\")?;\n461\t///\n462\t/// // Complete parsing and get result\n463\t/// let result = parser.finalize_parse()?;\n464\t/// ```\n465\tpub trait IncrementalParser<Output>: Parser<Vec<u8>, Output> {\n466\t    /// Initialize incremental parsing\n467\t    ///\n468\t    /// Called before feeding chunks. Sets up internal state for parsing.\n469\t    ///\n470\t    /// # Returns\n471\t    ///\n472\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Parser initialized successfully\n473\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Initialization failed\n474\t    fn init_parse(&mut self) -> Result<(), ParseError>;\n475\t\n476\t    /// Feed a chunk of input to the parser\n477\t    ///\n478\t    /// Processes a chunk of input without completing the parse. Can be\n479\t    /// called multiple times before finalization.\n480\t    ///\n481\t    /// # Arguments\n482\t    ///\n483\t    /// * `chunk` - Chunk of input data\n484\t    ///\n485\t    /// # Returns\n486\t    ///\n487\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Chunk processed successfully\n488\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Chunk processing failed\n489\t    fn feed_chunk(&mut self, chunk: Vec<u8>) -> Result<(), ParseError>;\n490\t\n491\t    /// Finalize parsing and return the result\n492\t    ///\n493\t    /// Called after all chunks have been fed. Completes the parse and\n494\t    /// returns the final result.\n495\t    ///\n496\t    /// # Returns\n497\t    ///\n498\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Parsing completed successfully\n499\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Finalization or parsing failed\n500\t    fn finalize_parse(&mut self) -> Result<Output, ParseError>;\n501\t}\n502\t\n503\t/// Options for customizing parser behavior\n504\t///\n505\t/// This structure provides configuration options that can be passed to\n506\t/// [`Parser::parse_with_options()`].\n507\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]\n508\tpub struct ParseOptions {\n509\t    /// Enable strict mode (disallow unknown fields, enforce types strictly)\n510\t    pub strict_mode: bool,\n511\t    /// Preserve comments in the output (if format supports it)\n512\t    pub preserve_comments: bool,\n513\t    /// Allow duplicate keys (if format normally disallows them)\n514\t    pub allow_duplicates: bool,\n515\t    /// Maximum depth for nested structures (0 = no limit)\n516\t    pub max_depth: usize,\n517\t    /// Custom delimiter for key-value pairs (None = use format default)\n518\t    pub delimiter: Option<char>,\n519\t}\n520\t\n521\timpl ParseOptions {\n522\t    /// Create new parse options with default values\n523\t    pub fn new() -> Self {\n524\t        Self::default()\n525\t    }\n526\t\n527\t    /// Create options for strict parsing\n528\t    ///\n529\t    /// Sets `strict_mode` to true and `allow_duplicates` to false.\n530\t    ///\n531\t    /// # Examples\n532\t    ///\n533\t    /// ```\n534\t    /// use armor::parsers::ParseOptions;\n535\t    ///\n536\t    /// let options = ParseOptions::strict();\n537\t    /// assert!(options.strict_mode);\n538\t    /// assert!(!options.allow_duplicates);\n539\t    /// ```\n540\t    pub fn strict() -> Self {\n541\t        Self {\n542\t            strict_mode: true,\n543\t            allow_duplicates: false,\n544\t            ..Default::default()\n545\t        }\n546\t    }\n547\t\n548\t    /// Create options for lenient parsing\n549\t    ///\n550\t    /// Sets `strict_mode` to false and `allow_duplicates` to true.\n551\t    ///\n552\t    /// # Examples\n553\t    ///\n554\t    /// ```\n555\t    /// use armor::parsers::ParseOptions;\n556\t    ///\n557\t    /// let options = ParseOptions::lenient();\n558\t    /// assert!(!options.strict_mode);\n559\t    /// assert!(options.allow_duplicates);\n560\t    /// ```\n561\t    pub fn lenient() -> Self {\n562\t        Self {\n563\t            strict_mode: false,\n564\t            allow_duplicates: true,\n565\t            ..Default::default()\n566\t        }\n567\t    }\n568\t\n569\t    /// Builder method: set strict mode\n570\t    pub fn with_strict_mode(mut self, strict: bool) -> Self {\n571\t        self.strict_mode = strict;\n572\t        self\n573\t    }\n574\t\n575\t    /// Builder method: set comment preservation\n576\t    pub fn with_preserve_comments(mut self, preserve: bool) -> Self {\n577\t        self.preserve_comments = preserve;\n578\t        self\n579\t    }\n580\t\n581\t    /// Builder method: set duplicate key allowance\n582\t    pub fn with_allow_duplicates(mut self, allow: bool) -> Self {\n583\t        self.allow_duplicates = allow;\n584\t        self\n585\t    }\n586\t\n587\t    /// Builder method: set maximum nesting depth\n588\t    pub fn with_max_depth(mut self, depth: usize) -> Self {\n589\t        self.max_depth = depth;\n590\t        self\n591\t    }\n592\t\n593\t    /// Builder method: set custom delimiter\n594\t    pub fn with_delimiter(mut self, delimiter: char) -> Self {\n595\t        self.delimiter = Some(delimiter);\n596\t        self\n597\t    }\n598\t}\n599\t\n600\t/// Metadata about a parser's capabilities\n601\t///\n602\t/// This structure describes what a parser can do, what formats it supports,\n603\t/// and its current configuration.\n604\t#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\n605\tpub struct ParseMetadata {\n606\t    /// Human-readable name of the parser (e.g., \"YAML Parser\", \"JSON Parser\")\n607\t    pub name: String,\n608\t    /// Format name this parser handles (e.g., \"YAML 1.2\", \"JSON\")\n609\t    pub format: String,\n610\t    /// Whether the parser supports streaming operations\n611\t    pub supports_streaming: bool,\n612\t    /// Whether the parser supports incremental parsing\n613\t    pub supports_incremental: bool,\n614\t    /// Supported file extensions (e.g., [\"yaml\", \"yml\"])\n615\t    pub extensions: Vec<String>,\n616\t    /// Parser version\n617\t    pub version: String,\n618\t}\n619\t\n620\timpl ParseMetadata {\n621\t    /// Create new parser metadata\n622\t    pub fn new(name: impl Into<String>, format: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n623\t        Self {\n624\t            name: name.into(),\n625\t            format: format.into(),\n626\t            supports_streaming: false,\n627\t            supports_incremental: false,\n628\t            extensions: Vec::new(),\n629\t            version: \"1.0.0\".to_string(),\n630\t        }\n631\t    }\n632\t\n633\t    /// Builder method: indicate streaming support\n634\t    pub fn with_streaming(mut self, supports: bool) -> Self {\n635\t        self.supports_streaming = supports;\n636\t        self\n637\t    }\n638\t\n639\t    /// Builder method: indicate incremental parsing support\n640\t    pub fn with_incremental(mut self, supports: bool) -> Self {\n641\t        self.supports_incremental = supports;\n642\t        self\n643\t    }\n644\t\n645\t    /// Builder method: add supported file extension\n646\t    pub fn with_extension(mut self, ext: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n647\t        self.extensions.push(ext.into());\n648\t        self\n649\t    }\n650\t\n651\t    /// Builder method: set parser version\n652\t    pub fn with_version(mut self, version: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n653\t        self.version = version.into();\n654\t        self\n655\t    }\n656\t\n657\t    /// Get the parser name\n658\t    pub fn name(&self) -> &str {\n659\t        &self.name\n660\t    }\n661\t\n662\t    /// Get the format name\n663\t    pub fn format(&self) -> &str {\n664\t        &self.format\n665\t    }\n666\t\n667\t    /// Check if streaming is supported\n668\t    pub fn supports_streaming(&self) -> bool {\n669\t        self.supports_streaming\n670\t    }\n671\t\n672\t    /// Check if incremental parsing is supported\n673\t    pub fn supports_incremental(&self) -> bool {\n674\t        self.supports_incremental\n675\t    }\n676\t\n677\t    /// Get supported file extensions\n678\t    pub fn extensions(&self) -> &[String] {\n679\t        &self.extensions\n680\t    }\n681\t\n682\t    /// Get parser version\n683\t    pub fn version(&self) -> &str {\n684\t        &self.version\n685\t    }\n686\t}\n687\t\n688\timpl Default for ParseMetadata {\n689\t    fn default() -> Self {\n690\t        Self {\n691\t            name: \"Generic Parser\".to_string(),\n692\t            format: \"Unknown\".to_string(),\n693\t            supports_streaming: false,\n694\t            supports_incremental: false,\n695\t            extensions: Vec::new(),\n696\t            version: \"1.0.0\".to_string(),\n697\t        }\n698\t    }\n699\t}\n700\t\n701\t/// Unified parse error type\n702\t///\n703\t/// This type wraps format-specific errors (e.g., `yaml::ParseError`)\n704\t/// into a common type that the generic `Parser` trait can use.\n705\t#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\n706\tpub /// Type mismatch error\n714\t    TypeMismatch {\n715\t        field: String,\n716\t        expected: String,\n717\t        actual: String,\n718\t    },\n719\t    /// Syntax error\n720\t {\n728\t        Self::Syntax(msg.into())\n729\t    }\n730\t\n731\t    /// Create an I/O error\n732\t    pub fn io(msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n733\t        Self::Io(msg.into())\n734\t    }\n735\t\n736\t    /// Create a validation error\n737\t    pub fn validation(msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n738\t        Self::Validation(msg.into())\n739\t    }\n740\t\n741\t    /// Create a type impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n752\t        Self::Other(msg.into())\n753\t    }\n754\t\n755\t    /// Check if this is a syntax error\n756\t    pub fn is_syntax(&self) -> bool {\n757\t        matches!(self, Self::Syntax(_))\n758\t            || matches!(self, Self::Yaml(e) if e.is_syntax())\n759\t    }\n760\t\n761\t    /// Check if this is an I/O error\n762\t    pub fn is_io(&self) -> bool {\n763\t        matches!(self, Self::Io(_))\n764\t            || matches!(self, Self::Yaml(e) if e.is_io())\n765\t    }\n766\t\n767\t    /// Check if this is a validation error\n768\t    pub fn e.summary(),\n783\t            Self::Io(msg) => format!(\"I/O error: {}\", msg),\n784\t            Self::Validation(msg) => format!(\"validation error: {}\", msg),\n785\t            Self::TypeMismatch { field, expected, actual } => {\n786\t                format!(\"type mismatch at '{}': expected {}, got {}\", field, expected, actual)\n787\t            }\n788\t            Self::Syntax(msg) =>  fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {\n796\t        write!(f, \"{}\", self.summary())\n797\t    }\n798\t}\n799\t\n800\timpl std::error::Error for ParseError Schema validation interface\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! This module defines the core [`Schema`] trait for validating structured data.\\n4\\t//! It provides a generic interface for implementing validation logic across different\\n5\\t//! data types and validation strategies.\\n6\\t//!\\n7\\t//! # Overview\\n8\\t//!\\n9\\t//! The [`Schema`] trait defines a contract for validating data structures. Implementors\\n10\\t//! can define domain-specific validation rules and provide detailed error information\\n11\\t//! when validation fails.\\n12\\t//!\\n13\\t//! # Basic Usage\\n14\\t//!\\n15\\t//! ```ignore\\n16\\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\\n17\\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n18\\t//!\\n19\\t//! struct PortSchema;\\n20\\t//!\\n21\\t//! impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\\n22\\t//!     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n23\\t//!         if *value == 0 {\\n24\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port cannot be 0\\\")\\n25\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n26\\t//!         }\\n27\\t//!         if *value > 65535 {\\n28\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port must be between 1 and 65535\\\")\\n29\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n30\\t//!         }\\n31\\t//!         Ok(())\\n32\\t//!     }\\n33\\t//! }\\n34\\t//! ```\\n35\\t//!\\n36\\t//! # Generic Validation\\n37\\t//!\\n38\\t//! The Schema trait is generic over the type being validated, allowing you to:\\n39\\t//!\\n40\\t//! - Validate primitive types (integers, strings, etc.)\\n41\\t//! - Validate complex structures (structs, enums)\\n42\\t//! - Validate collections (vectors, hash maps)\\n43\\t//! - Compose multiple validators together\\n44\\t//!\\n45\\t//! ```ignore\\n46\\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\\n47\\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n48\\t//!\\n49\\t//! // Validate a configuration struct\\n50\\t//! struct ConfigSchema;\\n51\\t//!\\n52\\t//! impl Schema<Config> for ConfigSchema {\\n53\\t//!     fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n54\\t//!         // Validate individual fields\\n55\\t//!         if config.port < 1 || config.port > 65535 {\\n56\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port out of range\\\")\\n57\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n58\\t//!         }\\n59\\t//!         if config.host.is_empty() {\\n60\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"host cannot be empty\\\")\\n61\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"host\\\"));\\n62\\t//!         }\\n63\\t//!         Ok(())\\n64\\t//!     }\\n65\\t//! }\\n66\\t//! ```\\n67\\t\\n68\\tuse crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n69\\t\\n70\\t/// Result type for validation operations\\n71\\t///\\n72\\t/// This is a type alias for `Result<(), ParseError>`, used throughout\\n73\\t/// the Schema trait and its implementations. This integrates the Schema\\n74\\t/// validation with the comprehensive error type hierarchy from the YAML parser.\\n75\\t///\\n76\\t/// # Error Types\\n77\\t///\\n78\\t/// The [`ParseError`] type provides rich error information including:\\n79\\t/// - Error categorization (syntax, validation, type mismatch, I/O, etc.)\\n80\\t/// - Location information (file path, line, column)\\n81\\t/// - Context messages and code snippets\\n82\\t/// - Builder pattern for adding contextual information\\n83\\t///\\n84\\t/// # Examples\\n85\\t///\\n86\\t/// ```\\n87\\t/// use armor::schema::{Schema, ValidationResult};\\n88\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n89\\t///\\n90\\t/// struct PortSchema;\\n91\\t///\\n92\\t/// impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\\n93\\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> ValidationResult {\\n94\\t///         if *value == 0 {\\n95\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port cannot be 0\\\")\\n96\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n97\\t///         }\\n98\\t///         if *value > 65535 {\\n99\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port must be between 1 and 65535\\\")\\n100\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n101\\t///         }\\n102\\t///         Ok(())\\n103\\t///     }\\n104\\t/// }\\n105\\t/// ```\\n106\\tpub type ValidationResult = Result<(), ParseError>;\\n107\\t\\n108\\t/// Schema validation trait\\n109\\t///\\n110\\t/// This trait defines a generic interface for validating structured data.\\n111\\t/// Implementors can define domain-specific validation rules for their data types.\\n112\\t///\\n113\\t/// # Type Parameters\\n114\\t///\\n115\\t/// * `T: ?Sized` - The type being validated (e.g., a struct, primitive type, or collection).\\n116\\t///   The `?Sized` bound allows the trait to be implemented for both sized and unsized types,\\n117\\t///   providing maximum flexibility for different validation scenarios.\\n118\\t///\\n119\\t/// # Required Methods\\n120\\t///\\n121\\t/// Implementors must define the [`validate`](Self::validate) method, which accepts\\n122\\t/// a reference to a value of type `T` and returns a [`ValidationResult`].\\n123\\t///\\n124\\t/// # Examples\\n125\\t///\\n126\\t/// ## Validating Primitive Types\\n127\\t///\\n128\\t/// ```\\n129\\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n130\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n131\\t///\\n132\\t/// struct PositiveIntegerSchema;\\n133\\t///\\n134\\t/// impl Schema<i32> for PositiveIntegerSchema {\\n135\\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n136\\t///         if *value <= 0 {\\n137\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"must be a positive }\\n222\\t/// }\\n223\\t/// ```\\n224\\tpub trait Schema<T: ?Sized> {\\n225\\t    /// Validate a value according to the schema rules\\n226\\t    ///\\n227\\t    /// This method validates the provided value and returns `Ok(())` if validation\\n228\\t    /// passes, or an error with details about what failed.\\n229\\t    ///\\n230\\t    /// # Arguments\\n231\\t    ///\\n232\\t    /// * `value` - A reference to the value being validated\\n233\\t    ///\\n234\\t    /// # Returns\\n235\\t    ///\\n236\\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Validation passed\\n237\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Validation failed with error details\\n238\\t    ///\\n239\\t    /// # Errors\\n240\\t    ///\\n241\\t    /// This method returns an error if:\\n242\\t    /// - Required fields are missing or empty\\n243\\t    /// - Values are outside allowed ranges\\n244\\t    /// - String values don't match required patterns\\n245\\t    /// - Any other schema constraint is violated\\n246\\t    ///\\n247\\t    /// # Examples\\n248\\t    ///\\n249\\t    /// ```\\n250\\t    /// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n251\\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n252\\t    ///\\n253\\t    /// struct RangeSchema }\\n267\\t    /// }\\n268\\t    ///\\n269\\t    /// let schema = RangeSchema { min: 1, max: 100 };\\n270\\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&50).is_ok());\\n271\\t    /// Generic parser traits and interfaces\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! This module defines the core parser traits that different parsing\\n4\\t//! strategies (YAML, JSON, etc.) can implement.\\n5\\t\\n6\\tuse std::path::Path;\\n7\\tuse crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError as YamlParseError;\\n8\\t\\n9\\t/// Generic parser trait for different parsing strategies\\n10\\t///\\n11\\t/// This trait defines the core interface for parsers that transform\\n12\\t/// input of one type into structured output. It is designed to be:\\n13\\t///\\n14\\t/// 1. **Generic** - Works with any input/output type combination\\n15\\t/// 2. **Composable** - Multiple parsers can be chained together\\n16\\t/// 3. **Extensible** - Supports streaming and incremental parsing\\n17\\t/// 4. **Error-aware** - Uses structured error types for consistent error handling\\n18\\t///\\n19\\t/// # Relationship to ParseResult and ParseError\\n20\\t///\\n21\\t/// The generic `Parser<Input, Output>` trait uses Rust's standard `Result<Output, ParseError>`\\n22\\t/// for its core parsing method. However, for YAML-specific parsing, the [`ParseResult<T>`](crate::parsers::yaml::ParseResult)\\n23\\t/// type provides a richer result structure that includes:\\n24\\t///\\n25\\t/// - **Metadata**: Lines processed, bytes processed, timing information\\n26\\t/// - **Warnings**: Non-fatal issues that don't prevent parsing (e.g., deprecated fields)\\n27\\t/// - **Detailed error context**: Location information, code snippets, suggestions\\n28\\t///\\n29\\t/// ## When to Use Result vs ParseResult\\n30\\t///\\n31\\t/// - **Use `Result<T, ParseError>`** (this trait): For generic parsers, simple parsing operations,\\n32\\t///   and when you want standard Rust error handling patterns\\n33\\t/// - **Use `ParseResult<T>`** (YAML-specific): For YAML parsing when you need rich metadata,\\n34\\t///   warning collection, and detailed error reporting\\n35\\t///\\n36\\t/// ## Converting Between Result and ParseResult\\n37\\t///\\n38\\t/// [`ParseResult<T>`](crate::parsers::yaml::ParseResult) implements `From<Result<T>>` for seamless\\n39\\t/// integration:\\n40\\t///\\n41\\t/// ```ignore\\n42\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseResult;\\n43\\t///\\n44\\t/// // Convert Result<T, ParseError> to ParseResult<T>\\n45\\t/// let result: Result<MyType, ParseError> = parse_value();\\n46\\t/// let parse_result: ParseResult<MyType> = ParseResult::from(result);\\n47\\t/// ```\\n48\\t///\\n49\\t/// # Type Parameters\\n50\\t///\\n51\\t/// * `Input` - The input source type (e.g., `&str`, `&[u8]`, `&Path`)\\n52\\t/// * `Output` - The parsed result type (e.g., configuration struct, AST)\\n53\\t///\\n54\\t/// # Trait Bounds\\n55\\t///\\n56\\t/// For common use cases, the following type combinations are typical:\\n57\\t///\\n58\\t/// - `Input = &str` for string-based formats (YAML, JSON, TOML)\\n59\\t/// - `Input = &[u8]` for binary formats\\n60\\t/// - `Input = &Path` for file-based parsing (delegates to string parsing)\\n61\\t///\\n62\\t/// The `Output` type is the target representation after parsing.\\n63\\t///\\n64\\t/// # Parsing Strategies\\n65\\t///\\n66\\t/// This trait supports multiple parsing strategies that can be configured through\\n67\\t/// [`ParseOptions`] or implementation-specific behavior:\\n68\\t///\\n69\\t/// ## Strict Parsing\\n70\\t///\\n71\\t/// Strict parsing follows format specifications precisely and rejects any deviations:\\n72\\t///\\n73\\t/// ```ignore\\n74\\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\\n75\\t///\\n76\\t/// let parser = StrictParser::new();\\n77\\t/// let options = ParseOptions::strict();\\n78\\t/// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\\n79\\t///\\n80\\t/// // In strict mode:\\n81\\t/// // - Unknown fields cause errors\\n82\\t/// // - Type mismatches are rejected\\n83\\t/// // - Duplicate keys are not allowed\\n84\\t/// // - Format specifications are enforced strictly\\n85\\t/// ```\\n86\\t///\\n87\\t/// ## Lenient Parsing\\n88\\t///\\n89\\t/// Lenient parsing is more forgiving and attempts to recover from errors:\\n90\\t///\\n91\\t/// ```ignore\\n92\\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\\n93\\t///\\n94\\t/// let parser = LenientParser::new();\\n95\\t/// let options = ParseOptions::lenient();\\n96\\t/// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\\n97\\t///\\n98\\t/// // In lenient mode:\\n99\\t/// // - Unknown fields are ignored\\n100\\t/// // - Type coercion is attempted\\n101\\t/// // - Duplicate keys use last value\\n102\\t/// // - Minor format issues are tolerated\\n103\\t/// ```\\n104\\t///\\n105\\t/// ## Custom Parsing\\n106\\t///\\n107\\t/// Custom parsers implement domain-specific parsing logic:\\n108\\t///\\n109\\t/// ```ignore\\n110\\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n111\\t///\\n112\\t/// struct CustomConfigParser {\\n113\\t///     allow_extended_syntax: bool,\\n114\\t///     resolve_variables: bool,\\n115\\t/// }\\n116\\t///\\n117\\t/// impl Parser<&str, CustomConfig> for CustomConfigParser {\\n118\\t///     fn parse(&self, source: &str) -> Result<CustomConfig, ParseError> {\\n119\\t///         // Custom parsing logic here\\n120\\t///         // - Variable substitution\\n121\\t///         // - Extended syntax support\\n122\\t///         // - Domain-specific validation\\n123\\t///         Ok(CustomConfig { /* ... */ })\\n124\\t///     }\\n125\\t/// }\\n126\\t/// ```\\n127\\t///\\n128\\t/// # Core Method\\n129\\t///\\n130\\t/// The [`Parser::parse()`] method is the core parsing operation:\\n131\\t///\\n132\\t/// ```ignore\\n133\\t/// fn parse(&self, source: Input) -> Result<Output, ParseError>\\n134\\t/// ```\\n135\\t///\\n136\\t/// # Examples\\n137\\t///\\n138\\t/// ## Basic String Parsing\\n139\\t///\\n140\\t/// ```ignore\\n141\\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n142\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n143\\t///\\n144\\t/// struct ConfigParser;\\n145\\t///\\n146\\t/// struct Config {\\n147\\t///     name: String,\\n148\\t///     value: i32,\\n149\\t/// }\\n150\\t///\\n151\\t/// impl Parser<&str, Config> for ConfigParser {\\n152\\t///     fn parse(&self, source: &str) -> Result<Config, ParseError> {\\n153\\t///         // Parse logic here\\n154\\t///         Ok(Config { name: \\\"test\\\".to_string(), value: 42 })\\n155\\t///     }\\n156\\t/// }\\n157\\t///\\n158\\t/// let parser = ConfigParser;\\n159\\t/// let config = parser.parse(\\\"name: test\\\\nvalue: 42\\\")?;\\n160\\t/// ```\\n161\\t///\\n162\\t/// ## Chaining Parsers\\n163\\t///\\n164\\t/// ```ignore\\n165\\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n166\\t///\\n167\\t/// // Parser 1: Raw string -> Intermediate representation\\n168\\t/// struct FirstParser;\\n169\\t/// impl Parser<&str, Intermediate> for FirstParser { /* ... */ }\\n170\\t///\\n171\\t/// // Parser 2: Intermediate -> Final output\\n172\\t/// struct SecondParser;\\n173\\t/// impl Parser<Intermediate, Output> for SecondParser { /* ... */ }\\n174\\t///\\n175\\t/// let first = FirstParser;\\n176\\t/// let second = SecondParser;\\n177\\t/// let output = second.parse(first.parse(input)?);\\n178\\t/// ```\\n179\\t///\\n180\\t/// ## Error Handling\\n181\\t///\\n182\\t/// ```ignore\\n183\\t/// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n184\\t///\\n185\\t/// match parser.parse(source) {\\n186\\t///     Ok(output) => println!(\\\"Parsed successfully: {:?}\\\", output),\\n187\\t///     Err(ParseError::Syntax(msg)) => eprintln!(\\\"Syntax error: {}\\\", msg),\\n188\\t///     Err(ParseError::Io(msg)) => eprintln!(\\\"I/O error: {}\\\", msg),\\n189\\t///     Err(e) => eprintln!(\\\"Other error: {}\\\", e),\\n190\\t/// }\\n191\\t/// ```\\n192\\tpub trait Parser<Input, Output> {\\n193\\t    /// Parse input and return the parsed result\\n194\\t    ///\\n195\\t    /// This method accepts input of type `Input` and attempts to parse it\\n196\\t    /// into a structured `Output` type. Returns `Ok(Output)` on success,\\n197\\t    /// or `Err(ParseError)` if parsing fails.\\n198\\t    ///\\n199\\t    /// # Arguments\\n200\\t    ///\\n201\\t    /// * `source` - The input source to parse\\n202\\t    ///\\n203\\t    /// # Returns\\n204\\t    ///\\n205\\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\\n206\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Parsing error with context\\n207\\t    ///\\n208\\t    /// # Errors\\n209\\t    ///\\n210\\t    /// This method will return an error if:\\n211\\t    /// - The input syntax is invalid (syntax error)\\n212\\t    /// - The input cannot be read (I/O error)\\n213\\t    /// - The input violates constraints (validation error)\\n214\\t    /// - Types don't match expectations (type mismatch)\\n215\\t    ///\\n216\\t    /// # Examples\\n217\\t    ///\\n218\\t    /// ```ignore\\n219\\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n220\\t    ///\\n221\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n222\\t    /// let result = parser.parse(\\\"key: value\\\")?;\\n223\\t    /// ```\\n224\\t    fn parse(&self, source: Input) -> Result<Output, ParseError>;\\n225\\t\\n226\\t    /// Parse input with extended options\\n227\\t    ///\\n228\\t    /// This method provides a way to customize parsing behavior through\\n229\\t    /// options. The default implementation delegates to [`Parser::parse()`].\\n230\\t    ///\\n231\\t    /// # Arguments\\n232\\t    ///\\n233\\t    /// * `source` - The input source to parse\\n234\\t    /// * `options` - Parsing options to customize behavior\\n235\\t    ///\\n236\\t    /// # Returns\\n237\\t    ///\\n238\\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\\n239\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Parsing error with context\\n240\\t    ///\\n241\\t    /// # Examples\\n242\\t    ///\\n243\\t    /// ```ignore\\n244\\t    /// use armor::parsers::{Parser, ParseOptions};\\n245\\t    ///\\n246\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n247\\t    /// let options = ParseOptions {\\n248\\t    ///     strict_mode: true,\\n249\\t    ///     preserve_comments: false,\\n250\\t    ///     ..Default::default()\\n251\\t    /// };\\n252\\t    /// let result = parser.parse_with_options(source, options)?;\\n253\\t    /// ```\\n254\\t    fn parse_with_options(&self, source: Input, options: ParseOptions) -> Result<Output, ParseError> {\\n255\\t        let _ = options; // Ignore options in default implementation\\n256\\t        self.parse(source)\\n257\\t    }\\n258\\t\\n259\\t    /// Parse a file at the given path\\n260\\t    ///\\n261\\t    /// Convenience method that reads a file and parses its contents.\\n262\\t    /// The default implementation reads the file and delegates to [`Parser::parse()`].\\n263\\t    ///\\n264\\t    /// # Arguments\\n265\\t    ///\\n266\\t    /// * `path` - Path to the file to parse\\n267\\t    ///\\n268\\t    /// # Returns\\n269\\t    ///\\n270\\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Successfully parsed result\\n271\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - I/O or parsing error\\n272\\t    ///\\n273\\t    /// # Errors\\n274\\t    ///\\n275\\t    /// This method will return an error if:\\n276\\t    /// - The file cannot be read (I/O error)\\n277\\t    /// - The file contents are invalid (parsing error)\\n278\\t    ///\\n279\\t    /// # Examples\\n280\\t    ///\\n281\\t    /// ```ignore\\n282\\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n283\\t    /// use std::path::Path;\\n284\\t    ///\\n285\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n286\\t    /// let result = parser.parse_file(Path::new(\\\"config.yaml\\\"))?;\\n287\\t    /// ```\\n288\\t    fn parse_file(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<Output, ParseError>\\n289\\t    where\\n290\\t        Self: Sized,\\n291\\t        Input: From<String>,\\n292\\t    {\\n293\\t        let content = std::fs::read_to_string(path)\\n294\\t            .map_err(|e| ParseError::Io(e.to_string()))?;\\n295\\t        self.parse(content.into())\\n296\\t    }\\n297\\t\\n298\\t    /// Validate input without fully parsing\\n299\\t    ///\\n300\\t    /// This method performs lightweight validation to check if the input\\n301\\t    /// is well-formed without constructing the full output structure.\\n302\\t    /// The default implementation attempts parsing and discards the result.\\n303\\t    ///\\n304\\t    /// # Arguments\\n305\\t    ///\\n306\\t    /// * `source` - The input source to validate\\n307\\t    ///\\n308\\t    /// # Returns\\n309\\t    ///\\n310\\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Input is valid\\n311\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Input is invalid\\n312\\t    ///\\n313\\t    /// # Examples\\n314\\t    ///\\n315\\t    /// ```ignore\\n316\\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n317\\t    ///\\n318\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n319\\t    /// if parser.validate(\\\"key: value\\\").is_ok() {\\n320\\t    ///     println!(\\\"Input is valid!\\\");\\n321\\t    /// }\\n322\\t    /// ```\\n323\\t    fn validate(&self, source: Input) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n324\\t        self.parse(source)?;\\n325\\t        Ok(())\\n326\\t    }\\n327\\t\\n328\\t    /// Get metadata about the parser\\n329\\t    ///\\n330\\t    /// Returns information about the parser's capabilities and configuration.\\n331\\t    ///\\n332\\t    /// # Returns\\n333\\t    ///\\n334\\t    /// Parser metadata including supported formats and features\\n335\\t    ///\\n336\\t    /// # Examples\\n337\\t    ///\\n338\\t    /// ```ignore\\n339\\t    /// use armor::parsers::Parser;\\n340\\t    ///\\n341\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n342\\t    /// let metadata = parser.metadata();\\n343\\t    /// println!(\\\"Parser: {}\\\", metadata.name());\\n344\\t    /// println!(\\\"Supports streaming: {}\\\", metadata.supports_streaming());\\n345\\t    /// ```\\n346\\t    fn metadata(&self) -> ParseMetadata {\\n347\\t        ParseMetadata::default()\\n348\\t    }\\n349\\t}\\n350\\t\\n351\\t/// Trait for parsers that support streaming input\\n352\\t///\\n353\\t/// This trait extends [`Parser`] with capabilities for processing large\\n354\\t/// inputs as a stream of chunks or multiple sources.\\n355\\t///\\n356\\t/// # Examples\\n357\\t///\\n358\\t/// ```ignore\\n359\\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, StreamingParser};\\n360\\t///\\n361\\t/// let parser = MyStreamingParser::new();\\n362\\t///\\n363\\t/// // Parse multiple sources\\n364\\t/// let sources = vec![\\\"file1.yaml\\\", \\\"file2.yaml\\\", \\\"file3.yaml\\\"];\\n365\\t/// let results = parser.parse_stream(sources)?;\\n366\\t///\\n367\\t/// for result in results {\\n368\\t///     println!(\\\"Parsed: {:?}\\\", result);\\n369\\t/// }\\n370\\t/// ```\\n371\\tpub trait StreamingParser<Input, Output>: Parser<Input, Output> {\\n372\\t    /// Parse multiple sources in sequence\\n373\\t    ///\\n374\\t    /// This method processes a collection of input sources and returns\\n375\\t    /// a vector of parsed results. Errors from individual sources are\\n376\\t    /// collected and returned together.\\n377\\t    ///\\n378\\t    /// # Arguments\\n379\\t    ///\\n380\\t    /// * `sources` - Iterator of input sources to parse\\n381\\t    ///\\n382\\t    /// # Returns\\n383\\t    ///\\n384\\t    /// * `Ok(Vec<Output>)` - All sources parsed successfully\\n385\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - One or more sources failed to parse\\n386\\t    ///\\n387\\t    /// # Examples\\n388\\t    ///\\n389\\t    /// ```ignore\\n390\\t    /// use armor::parsers::StreamingParser;\\n391\\t    ///\\n392\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n393\\t    /// let sources = vec![\\\"config1.yaml\\\", \\\"config2.yaml\\\"];\\n394\\t    /// let results = parser.parse_stream(sources)?;\\n395\\t    /// ```\\n396\\t    fn parse_stream<'a, I>(\\n397\\t        &self,\\n398\\t        sources: I,\\n399\\t    ) -> Result<Vec<Output>, ParseError>\\n400\\t    where\\n401\\t        Input: 'a,\\n402\\t        I: IntoIterator<Item = Input>;\\n403\\t\\n404\\t    /// Parse sources in parallel\\n405\\t    ///\\n406\\t    /// This method processes multiple sources concurrently using parallel\\n407\\t    /// execution. Results are collected in the order of the input iterator.\\n408\\t    ///\\n409\\t    /// # Arguments\\n410\\t    ///\\n411\\t    /// * `sources` - Iterator of input sources to parse\\n412\\t    /// * `parallelism` - Maximum number of concurrent parse operations\\n413\\t    ///\\n414\\t    /// # Returns\\n415\\t    ///\\n416\\t    /// * `Ok(Vec<Output>)` - All sources parsed successfully\\n417\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - One or more sources failed to parse\\n418\\t    ///\\n419\\t    /// # Examples\\n420\\t    ///\\n421\\t    /// ```ignore\\n422\\t    /// use armor::parsers::StreamingParser;\\n423\\t    ///\\n424\\t    /// let parser = MyParser::new();\\n425\\t    /// let sources = vec![\\\"file1.yaml\\\", \\\"file2.yaml\\\", \\\"file3.yaml\\\"];\\n426\\t    /// let results = parser.parse_parallel(sources, 4)?;\\n427\\t    /// ```\\n428\\t    fn parse_parallel<'a, I>(\\n429\\t        &self,\\n430\\t        sources: I,\\n431\\t        parallelism: usize,\\n432\\t    ) -> Result<Vec<Output>, ParseError>\\n433\\t    where\\n434\\t        Input: 'a,\\n435\\t        I: IntoIterator<Item = Input>,\\n436\\t    {\\n437\\t        // Default implementation processes sequentially\\n438\\t        self.parse_stream(sources)\\n439\\t    }\\n440\\t}\\n441\\t\\n442\\t/// Trait for parsers that support incremental parsing\\n443\\t///\\n444\\t/// This trait extends [`Parser`] with the ability to parse input in chunks,\\n445\\t/// allowing processing of large inputs without loading the entire source\\n446\\t/// into memory.\\n447\\t///\\n448\\t/// # Examples\\n449\\t///\\n450\\t/// ```ignore\\n451\\t/// use armor::parsers::{Parser, IncrementalParser};\\n452\\t///\\n453\\t/// let parser = MyIncrementalParser::new();\\n454\\t///\\n455\\t/// // Initialize parsing\\n456\\t/// parser.init_parse()?;\\n457\\t///\\n458\\t/// // Feed chunks incrementally\\n459\\t/// parser.feed_chunk(b\\\"key: \\\")?;\\n460\\t/// parser.feed_chunk(b\\\"value\\\")?;\\n461\\t///\\n462\\t/// // Complete parsing and get result\\n463\\t/// let result = parser.finalize_parse()?;\\n464\\t/// ```\\n465\\tpub trait IncrementalParser<Output>: Parser<Vec<u8>, Output> {\\n466\\t    /// Initialize incremental parsing\\n467\\t    ///\\n468\\t    /// Called before feeding chunks. Sets up internal state for parsing.\\n469\\t    ///\\n470\\t    /// # Returns\\n471\\t    ///\\n472\\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Parser initialized successfully\\n473\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Initialization failed\\n474\\t    fn init_parse(&mut self) -> Result<(), ParseError>;\\n475\\t\\n476\\t    /// Feed a chunk of input to the parser\\n477\\t    ///\\n478\\t    /// Processes a chunk of input without completing the parse. Can be\\n479\\t    /// called multiple times before finalization.\\n480\\t    ///\\n481\\t    /// # Arguments\\n482\\t    ///\\n483\\t    /// * `chunk` - Chunk of input data\\n484\\t    ///\\n485\\t    /// # Returns\\n486\\t    ///\\n487\\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Chunk processed successfully\\n488\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Chunk processing failed\\n489\\t    fn feed_chunk(&mut self, chunk: Vec<u8>) -> Result<(), ParseError>;\\n490\\t\\n491\\t    /// Finalize parsing and return the result\\n492\\t    ///\\n493\\t    /// Called after all chunks have been fed. Completes the parse and\\n494\\t    /// returns the final result.\\n495\\t    ///\\n496\\t    /// # Returns\\n497\\t    ///\\n498\\t    /// * `Ok(Output)` - Parsing completed successfully\\n499\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Finalization or parsing failed\\n500\\t    fn finalize_parse(&mut self) -> Result<Output, ParseError>;\\n501\\t}\\n502\\t\\n503\\t/// Options for customizing parser behavior\\n504\\t///\\n505\\t/// This structure provides configuration options that can be passed to\\n506\\t/// [`Parser::parse_with_options()`].\\n507\\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]\\n508\\tpub struct ParseOptions {\\n509\\t    /// Enable strict mode (disallow unknown fields, enforce types strictly)\\n510\\t    pub strict_mode: bool,\\n511\\t    /// Preserve comments in the output (if format nested structures (0 = no limit)\\n516\\t    pub max_depth: usize,\\n517\\t    /// Custom delimiter for key-value pairs (None = use format default)\\n518\\t    pub delimiter: }\\n526\\t\\n527\\t    /// Create options for strict parsing\\n528\\t    ///\\n529\\t    /// Sets `strict_mode` to true and `allow_duplicates` to false.\\n530\\t    ///\\n531\\t    /// # Schema validation interface\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! This module defines the core [`Schema`] trait for validating structured data.\\n4\\t//! It provides a generic interface for implementing validation logic across different\\n5\\t//! data types and validation strategies.\\n6\\t//!\\n7\\t//! # Overview\\n8\\t//!\\n9\\t//! The [`Schema`] trait defines a contract for validating data structures. Implementors\\n10\\t//! can define domain-specific validation rules and provide detailed error information\\n11\\t//! when validation fails.\\n12\\t//!\\n13\\t//! # Basic Usage\\n14\\t//!\\n15\\t//! ```ignore\\n16\\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\\n17\\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n18\\t//!\\n19\\t//! struct PortSchema;\\n20\\t//!\\n21\\t//! impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\\n22\\t//!     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n23\\t//!         if *value == 0 {\\n24\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port cannot be 0\\\")\\n25\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n26\\t//!         }\\n27\\t//!         if *value > 65535 {\\n28\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port must be between 1 and 65535\\\")\\n29\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n30\\t//!         }\\n31\\t//!         Ok(())\\n32\\t//!     }\\n33\\t//! }\\n34\\t//! ```\\n35\\t//!\\n36\\t//! # Generic Validation\\n37\\t//!\\n38\\t//! The Schema trait is generic over the type being validated, allowing you to:\\n39\\t//!\\n40\\t//! - Validate primitive types (integers, strings, etc.)\\n41\\t//! - Validate complex structures (structs, enums)\\n42\\t//! - Validate collections (vectors, hash maps)\\n43\\t//! - Compose multiple validators together\\n44\\t//!\\n45\\t//! ```ignore\\n46\\t//! use armor::schema::Schema;\\n47\\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n48\\t//!\\n49\\t//! // Validate a configuration struct\\n50\\t//! struct ConfigSchema;\\n51\\t//!\\n52\\t//! impl Schema<Config> for ConfigSchema {\\n53\\t//!     fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n54\\t//!         // Validate individual fields\\n55\\t//!         if config.port < 1 || config.port > 65535 {\\n56\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port out of range\\\")\\n57\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n58\\t//!         }\\n59\\t//!         if config.host.is_empty() {\\n60\\t//!             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"host cannot be empty\\\")\\n61\\t//!                 .with_path(\\\"host\\\"));\\n62\\t//!         }\\n63\\t//!         Ok(())\\n64\\t//!     }\\n65\\t//! }\\n66\\t//! ```\\n67\\t\\n68\\tuse crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n69\\t\\n70\\t/// Result type for validation operations\\n71\\t///\\n72\\t/// This is a type alias for `Result<(), ParseError>`, used throughout\\n73\\t/// the Schema trait and its implementations. This integrates the Schema\\n74\\t/// validation with the comprehensive error type hierarchy from the YAML parser.\\n75\\t///\\n76\\t/// # Error Types\\n77\\t///\\n78\\t/// The [`ParseError`] type provides rich error information including:\\n79\\t/// - Error categorization (syntax, validation, type mismatch, I/O, etc.)\\n80\\t/// - Location information (file path, line, column)\\n81\\t/// - Context messages and code snippets\\n82\\t/// - Builder pattern for adding contextual information\\n83\\t///\\n84\\t/// # Examples\\n85\\t///\\n86\\t/// ```\\n87\\t/// use armor::schema::{Schema, ValidationResult};\\n88\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n89\\t///\\n90\\t/// struct PortSchema;\\n91\\t///\\n92\\t/// impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\\n93\\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &u16) -> ValidationResult {\\n94\\t///         if *value == 0 {\\n95\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port cannot be 0\\\")\\n96\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n97\\t///         }\\n98\\t///         if *value > 65535 {\\n99\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"port must be between 1 and 65535\\\")\\n100\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n101\\t///         }\\n102\\t///         Ok(())\\n103\\t///     }\\n104\\t/// }\\n105\\t/// ```\\n106\\tpub type ValidationResult = Result<(), ParseError>;\\n107\\t\\n108\\t/// Schema validation trait\\n109\\t///\\n110\\t/// This trait defines a generic interface for validating structured data.\\n111\\t/// Implementors can define domain-specific validation rules for their data types.\\n112\\t///\\n113\\t/// # Type Parameters\\n114\\t///\\n115\\t/// * `T: ?Sized` - The type being validated (e.g., a struct, primitive type, or collection).\\n116\\t///   The `?Sized` bound allows the trait to be implemented for both sized and unsized types,\\n117\\t///   providing maximum flexibility for different validation scenarios.\\n118\\t///\\n119\\t/// # Required Methods\\n120\\t///\\n121\\t/// Implementors must define the [`validate`](Self::validate) method, which accepts\\n122\\t/// a reference to a value of type `T` and returns a [`ValidationResult`].\\n123\\t///\\n124\\t/// # Examples\\n125\\t///\\n126\\t/// ## Validating Primitive Types\\n127\\t///\\n128\\t/// ```\\n129\\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n130\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n131\\t///\\n132\\t/// struct PositiveIntegerSchema;\\n133\\t///\\n134\\t/// impl Schema<i32> for PositiveIntegerSchema {\\n135\\t///     fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n136\\t///         if *value <= 0 {\\n137\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"must be a positive integer\\\")\\n138\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"value\\\"));\\n139\\t///         }\\n140\\t///         Ok(())\\n141\\t///     }\\n142\\t/// }\\n143\\t///\\n144\\t/// let schema = PositiveIntegerSchema;\\n145\\t/// assert!(schema.validate(&42).is_ok());\\n146\\t/// assert!(schema.validate(&-5).is_err());\\n147\\t/// ```\\n148\\t///\\n149\\t/// ## Validating Structs\\n150\\t///\\n151\\t/// ```ignore\\n152\\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n153\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n154\\t///\\n155\\t/// struct ServerConfig {\\n156\\t///     host: String,\\n157\\t///     port: u16,\\n158\\t/// }\\n159\\t///\\n160\\t/// struct ServerConfigSchema;\\n161\\t///\\n162\\t/// impl Schema<ServerConfig> for ServerConfigSchema {\\n163\\t///     fn validate(&self, config: &ServerConfig) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n164\\t///         if config.host.is_empty() {\\n165\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"cannot be empty\\\")\\n166\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"host\\\"));\\n167\\t///         }\\n168\\t///         if config.port == 0 {\\n169\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"cannot be 0\\\")\\n170\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n171\\t///         }\\n172\\t///         Ok(())\\n173\\t///     }\\n174\\t/// }\\n175\\t/// ```\\n176\\t///\\n177\\t/// ## Composing Validators\\n178\\t///\\n179\\t/// ```ignore\\n180\\t/// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n181\\t/// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n182\\t///\\n183\\t/// struct Config {\\n184\\t///     name: String,\\n185\\t///     port: u16,\\n186\\t/// }\\n187\\t///\\n188\\t/// // Individual field validators\\n189\\t/// struct NameSchema;\\n190\\t/// impl Schema<String> for NameSchema {\\n191\\t///     fn validate(&self, name: &String) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n192\\t///         if name.len() < 3 {\\n193\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"must be at least 3 characters\\\")\\n194\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"name\\\"));\\n195\\t///         }\\n196\\t///         Ok(())\\n197\\t///     }\\n198\\t/// }\\n199\\t///\\n200\\t/// struct PortSchema;\\n201\\t/// impl Schema<u16> for PortSchema {\\n202\\t///     fn validate(&self, port: &u16) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n203\\t///         if *port == 0 {\\n204\\t///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\\"cannot be 0\\\")\\n205\\t///                 .with_path(\\\"port\\\"));\\n206\\t///         }\\n207\\t///         Ok(())\\n208\\t///     }\\n209\\t/// }\\n210\\t///\\n211\\t/// // Composite validator\\n212\\t/// struct ConfigSchema;\\n213\\t/// impl Schema<Config> for ConfigSchema {\\n214\\t///     fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n215\\t///         // Delegate to field-specific validators\\n216\\t///         NameSchema.validate(&config.name)\\n217\\t///             .map_err(|e| e.with_path(\\\"name\\\"))?;\\n218\\t///         PortSchema.validate(&config.port)\\n219\\t///             .map_err(|e| e.with_path(\\\"port\\\"))?;\\n220\\t///         Ok(())\\n221\\t///     }\\n222\\t/// }\\n223\\t/// ```\\n224\\tpub trait Schema<T: ?Sized> {\\n225\\t    /// Validate a value according to the schema rules\\n226\\t    ///\\n227\\t    /// This method validates the provided value and returns `Ok(())` if validation\\n228\\t    /// passes, or an error with details about what failed.\\n229\\t    ///\\n230\\t    /// # Arguments\\n231\\t    ///\\n232\\t    /// * `value` - A reference to the value being validated\\n233\\t    ///\\n234\\t    /// # Returns\\n235\\t    ///\\n236\\t    /// * `Ok(())` - Validation passed\\n237\\t    /// * `Err(ParseError)` - Validation failed with error details\\n238\\t    ///\\n239\\t    /// # Errors\\n240\\t    ///\\n241\\t    /// This method returns an error if:\\n242\\t    /// - Required fields are missing or empty\\n243\\t    /// - Values are outside allowed ranges\\n244\\t    /// - String values don't match required patterns\\n245\\t    /// - Any other schema constraint is violated\\n246\\t    ///\\n247\\t    /// # Examples\\n248\\t    ///\\n249\\t    /// ```\\n250\\t    /// use armor::schema::Schema;\\n251\\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n252\\t    ///\\n253\\t    /// struct RangeSchema {\\n254\\t    ///     min: i32,\\n255\\t    ///     max: i32,\\n256\\t    /// }\\n257\\t    ///\\n258\\t    /// impl Schema<i32> for RangeSchema {\\n259\\t    ///     fn validate(&self, value: &i32) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\\n260\\t    ///         if *value < self.min || *value > self.max {\\n261\\t    ///             return Err(ParseError::validation(\\n262\\t    ///                 &format!(\\\"must be between {} and {}\\\", self.min, self.max)\\n263\\t    ///             ).with_path(\\\"value\\\"));\\n264\\t    ///         }\\n265\\t    ///         Ok(())\\n266\\t    ///     }\\n267\\t    /// }\\n268\\t    ///\\n269\\t    /// let schema = RangeSchema { min: 1, max: 100 };\\n270\\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&50).is_ok());\\n271\\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&0).is_err());\\n272\\t    /// assert!(schema.validate(&101).is_err());\\n273\\t    /// ```\\n274\\t    fn validate(&self, value: &T) -> ValidationResult;\\n275\\t}\\n276\\t\\n277\\t#[cfg(test)]\\n278\\tmod tests {\\n279\\t    use super::*;\\n280\\t    use crate::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\\n281\\t\\n282\\t    #[test]\\n283\\t    fn \\\");\\n441\\t        assert!(result.is_err());\\n442\\t        assert!(result.unwrap_err().is_validation());\\n443\\t    }\\n444\\t\\n445\\t    #[test]\\n446\\t    fn test_generic_vec_validation()
